did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) enhanced by the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) for skin cancer detection. The AI method involves optimizing hyperparameters (dropout rate and learning rate) of the CNN using the metaheuristic GWO algorithm to classify dermatoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset into seven skin lesion categories, achieving 87% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study proposing an enhanced CNN model optimized by the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) for skin cancer detection. It details the methodology (using the HAM10000 dataset, data augmentation, and hyperparameter optimization), reports specific performance metrics (87% accuracy, precision, recall, F1-scores), and compares these new results against prior studies. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICATEI), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing existing literature.

title: An Enhanced CNN with Grey Wolf Optimizer for Skin Cancer Detection
journal: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies in Energy and Informatic (ICATEI)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer detection by enhancing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) using the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) to optimize hyperparameters such as dropout rate and learning rate. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset, comprising over 10,000 dermatoscopic images across seven skin lesion categories, and applied data augmentation techniques including rotation, flipping, and noise addition to handle class imbalance. The proposed model was evaluated using accuracy, loss, confusion matrix, and F1-score metrics on a split of training, validation, and testing data. The enhanced CNN achieved an overall accuracy of 87%, with specific F1-scores ranging from 0.77 for Benign keratosis-like lesions to 1.00 for Vascular lesions. This result exceeds previous benchmarks, which typically ranged between 70% and 83%, demonstrating the effectiveness of GWO in improving CNN performance and generalization for skin cancer classification.

all_task_types.target: Seven skin lesion categories (Actinic keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, and Vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The model performs multi-class classification of dermatoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset into seven discrete diagnostic categories using a CNN with SoftMax activation for the final dense layer. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores for each class.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermatoscopic images obtained from Kaggle, consisting of over 10,000 images categorized into seven skin lesion types. The dataset was originally imbalanced and required data augmentation (rotation, flipping, noise addition) to address class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model after data augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (split was 15% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance (split was 15% of the dataset).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores, and compared against prior studies based on these metrics without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.dataset_description: Public dataset of dermatoscopic images obtained from Kaggle, consisting of 10,015 images categorized into seven skin lesion types. Split into Training (70%), Validation (15%), and Testing (15%).
HAM10000.experiment: Enhanced CNN with Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) for hyperparameter optimization (dropout rate and learning rate)
HAM10000.notes: The model achieved an overall accuracy of 87%, exceeding previous benchmarks which ranged between 70% and 83%. The study notes that Melanocytic nevi had the most accurate predictions (976 samples), while Benign keratosis-like lesions and Melanoma showed lower F1-scores due to overlapping characteristics. The confusion matrix and training/validation loss curves indicated effective generalization without overfitting.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) optimized by Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) optimized by Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO)
CNN enhanced with Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images from original 600x450 pixels to 100x100 pixels
Optional exponential transformation to boost image contrast

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Randomized rotation of images within a range of -45 to 45 degrees.
Mirroring images along the horizontal axis with a 50% probability.
Introduction of random Gaussian noise with a specified scale to enhance sample variability.
A custom function (exp transform) used to boost image contrast; noted as optional/not mandatory.

conclusions: The Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) effectively enhances the performance of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for skin cancer detection by optimizing critical hyperparameters, specifically dropout rate and learning rate.
The proposed CNN-GWO model achieved an overall accuracy of 87% on the HAM10000 dataset, exceeding previous benchmarks which typically ranged between 70% and 83%.

The model demonstrated strong generalization capabilities, with validation accuracy stabilizing between 0.85 and 0.9 and loss converging after approximately 100 epochs, indicating effective prevention of overfitting.

The model achieved perfect classification (F1-score of 1.00) for Vascular lesions and high performance for Dermatofibroma (F1-score of 0.97), likely due to distinct visual characteristics or adequate dataset representation.

The model exhibited lower performance for Benign keratosis-like lesions (F1-score of 0.77) and Melanoma (F1-score of 0.80), attributed to overlapping visual characteristics between these classes and potential class imbalance in the training data.

Metaheuristic optimization techniques like GWO offer a robust alternative to manual hyperparameter tuning, which is resource-intensive and prone to human error, thereby improving the persuasiveness and flexibility of AI-based skin cancer detection models.

Future improvements could involve expanding the dataset for underrepresented classes, enhancing feature extraction methods, and addressing misclassifications between visually similar categories to further increase accuracy.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have class imbalance and demographic biases (predominantly fair-skinned populations), limiting the generalizability of the 87% accuracy to diverse real-world clinical populations.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on a single internal test split (15% of HAM10000), raising concerns about overfitting to this specific dataset distribution.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance improvements over baseline models, relying instead on point estimates.

Performance varies significantly by class: Vascular lesions achieved a perfect F1-score of 1.00, while Benign keratosis-like lesions had the lowest F1-score (0.77), indicating potential clinical risk for misdiagnosis of this specific benign condition.

Confusion matrix analysis reveals specific misclassification risks, such as 78 samples of Benign keratosis-like lesions being incorrectly classified as Melanoma, which could lead to unnecessary biopsies in a clinical setting.

The dataset was artificially balanced using data augmentation (rotation, flipping, noise) for minority classes; while this improved generalization metrics, it may not fully replicate the prevalence and variability of rare lesions in actual clinical practice.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (ICATEI) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to top-tier medical AI journals.

The model architecture is relatively small (approx. 8.6 million parameters) and input images were downsampled to 100x100 pixels, which may discard fine-grained dermatological features critical for distinguishing subtle malignant patterns.
